questions.question: Explain the difference between a single-cycle and multi-cycle processor datapath implementation. What are the trade-offs involved?
keyPoints: Single-cycle: one instruction completes per clock cycle, multi-cycle: instruction takes multiple cycles
Single-cycle requires combinational control logic, multi-cycle uses sequential state machine
Single-cycle has higher clock period (limited by slowest instruction), multi-cycle has lower clock frequency
Multi-cycle allows reusing functional units, reducing hardware cost
Multi-cycle has higher CPI (average >1) but can achieve higher clock frequency
Trade-off: Single-cycle simplicity vs multi-cycle efficiency and hardware optimization

questions.question: Define Register Transfer Notation (RTN) and illustrate with examples how it represents datapath operations for an arithmetic instruction.
keyPoints: RTN provides formal, unambiguous description of microoperations
Example for ADD R3, R1, R2: R3 ← R1 + R2
Shows data flow: register read → ALU operation → register write
Microoperations are atomic operations executing in one clock cycle
Multiple microoperations can occur simultaneously if in different datapath components
RTN forms basis for control signal generation and datapath design

questions.question: What are the three types of pipeline hazards? Explain each with an example and suggest a method to resolve them.
keyPoints: Structural hazard: hardware cannot support instruction overlap -解决方法: duplicate hardware or stall
RAW (true dependency): instruction depends on result not yet written -解决方法: forwarding, stalls, compiler scheduling
WAR/WAW (in-order pipelines): not common in simple pipelines but exist -解决方法: register renaming, out-of-order execution
Control hazard: branch outcome unknown -解决方法: branch prediction, delay slots, flush on misprediction

questions.question: Derive the formula for pipeline speedup and explain why the speedup is limited even with perfect hazard handling.
keyPoints: Non-pipelined: n instructions take n×k cycles (k stages, 1 cycle each)
Pipelined: n instructions take k + (n-1) cycles
Speedup S = (n×k)/(k + n-1)
As n→∞, S → k (number of pipeline stages)
Limitation: pipeline fill and drain overhead (k-1 cycles)
This demonstrates Amdahl's law for pipelining - inherent sequential portion limits parallelization
Diminishing returns from deeper pipelines for typical program lengths

questions.question: Explain the role of the Control Unit in instruction execution. How does it generate control signals for the datapath?
keyPoints: Control Unit interprets instruction opcode to determine operation type
Generates timing signals for each stage of instruction execution
For R-type: RegWrite, ALUOp, RegDst signals control datapath
For load/store: MemRead, MemWrite, MemtoReg signals enable memory
For branch: PCSrc signal determines next PC value
Implementation: either hardwired (combinational) or microprogrammed (sequenced)

questions.question: A processor has the following instruction mix: 40% ALU (CPI=1), 30% Load (CPI=2), 20% Store (CPI=2), 10% Branch (CPI=3). Calculate the average CPI and total execution time for 1 million instructions with 2GHz clock.
keyPoints: Average CPI = Σ(frequency × CPI) = 0.4×1 + 0.3×2 + 0.2×2 + 0.1×3
Clock period = 1/2GHz = 0.5 ns
Execution time = (Instruction count × CPI × Clock period)
Time = 10^6 × 1.7 × 0.5 ns = 0.85 seconds = 850 ms
Shows impact of instruction mix on overall performance

questions.question: What is forwarding in pipeline processors? Explain the forwarding paths required for a 5-stage pipeline to handle RAW hazards.
keyPoints: Forwarding (bypassing) routes results directly from pipeline registers to later stages
ELU result can be forwarded from EX/MEM pipeline register to next instruction's EX stage
MEM/WB register value can be forwarded to EX stage for dependency
Load result forwarded from MEM/WB to EX of following instruction (1-cycle delay needed)
Requires additional multiplexers at ALU inputs to select forwarded or register file data
Forwarding eliminates most stalls for arithmetic dependencies

questions.question: Design a control signal sequence for executing the instruction 'LOAD R1, 100(R2)' in a 5-stage pipeline. Show which control signals are asserted in each stage and explain the data flow.
keyPoints: IF: PC → MAR, MemRead, IR ← M[MAR], PC ← PC + 4
ID: R2 read from register file, sign-extend offset 100
EX: ALU computes effective address: R2 + offset, store in EX/MEM register
MEM: MemRead with effective address, load data into MEM/WB pipeline register
WB: RegWrite, write data from MEM/WB to R1 in register file
Control signals: PCSrc=0, RegDst=0, MemRead=1, MemtoReg=1, ALUOp=add, RegWrite=1
Shows how memory-address computation and memory access occur in sequence

questions.question: A 5-stage pipeline has a branch misprediction penalty of 2 cycles. If branches are 20% of instructions and 40% of branches are mispredicted, calculate: (a) effective CPI (b) speedup over non-pipelined processor (c) how many stall cycles per 1000 instructions
keyPoints: (a) Branch penalty = branch_freq × mispredict_rate × flush = 0.2 × 0.4 × 2 = 0.16
Effective CPI = Ideal CPI (1) + branch_penalty = 1 + 0.16 = 1.16
(b) Non-pipelined CPI = 5 (5 stages), Speedup = 5 / 1.16 = 4.31×
(c) Stall cycles = branch_penalty × n = 0.16 × 1000 = 160 cycles
Additional: With data forwarding and other optimizations, CPI could be reduced further
Demonstrates impact of control hazards on pipeline performance

questions.question: Compare hardwired control versus microprogrammed control for processor implementation. Under what conditions would you choose each approach?
keyPoints: Hardwired: control signals generated by combinational logic from opcode, faster but less flexible
Microprogrammed: control signals stored in control memory (ROM/PROM), more flexible, easier to modify
Hardwired advantages: faster (no microinstruction fetch), suitable for RISC with simple instruction set
Microprogrammed advantages: easier to implement complex instruction sets, easier to modify/extend, used in CISC
Hybrid approaches exist combining both methods
Choice factors: instruction set complexity, design time, flexibility needed, performance requirements
Modern RISC typically uses hardwired; microprogramming useful for emulation and complex architectures
